{{Short description|Burial mound in Japan}} {{Infobox ancient site | name =Sōja Futagoyama Kofun | native_name =総社二子山古墳 | native_name_lang = | image = Maebashi Souzya-hutagoyama Kohun 1.jpg | image_size = | alt = | caption = Sōja Futagoyama Kofun | map_type = Japan Gunma Prefecture#Japan | map_alt = Location in Japan | relief = 1 | map_caption = Sōja Futagoyama Kofun | map_size = 270px | altitude_m = | altitude_ref = | coordinates = {{coord|36|24|41|N|139|2|5.3|E|region:JP|display=inline}} | map_dot_label = | location = [[Maebashi, Gunma]], [[Japan]] | region = [[Kantō region]] | type = [[kofun]] | part_of = | length = | width = | area = | volume = | diameter = | circumference = | height = | built =late 6th century | abandoned = | epochs =[[Kofun period]] | cultures = | discovered = | excavations = | archaeologists = | condition = | ownership = | management = | public_access = Yes (Park) | other_designation = | website = <!-- {{URL|example.com}} --> | architectural_styles = | architectural_details = |notes = {{box|background=white|align=center|wide=yes|border size=3px|border color=brown|text align=center|[[Monuments of Japan|National Historic Site of Japan]]}} }}
{{nihongo|'''Sōja Futagoyama Kofun'''|総社二子山古墳|}} is a [[Kofun period]] [[kofun|burial mound]] located in what is now the city of [[Maebashi, Gunma|Maebashi]], [[Gunma Prefecture]] in the northern [[Kantō region]] of [[Japan]]. It was designated a [[Monuments of Japan|National Historic Site of Japan]] in 1927.<ref name="Bunka1">{{cite web |url=https://bunka.nii.ac.jp/heritages/detail/171625|title=二子山古墳|language=ja |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] }}</ref> It is also sometimes referred to as the {{nihongo|'''Dantaiyama Kofun'''|男体山古墳|}}.
==Overview== Sōja Futagoyama Kofun is a {{nihongo|''[[zenpō-kōen-fun]]''|前方後円墳}}, which is shaped like a keyhole, having one square end and one circular end, when viewed from above. The tumulus is located on a plain near the southeastern slopes of [[Mount Haruna]], and is one of a cluster of ''kofun'' tumuli in the area. It has a total length of 89.9 meters and was built in two tiers, orientated to the west. The surface was originally covered in ''[[fukiishi]]'' with rows of cylindrical ''[[haniwa]]''. From the topography of the site, it is believed that the tumulus was surrounded by a moat, although this has not yet been confirmed via [[archaeological excavation]]. Although the detailed structure of the mound has not been investigated yet, it is known to have two lateral [[burial chamber]]s, one in the posterior circular portion and one in the anterior rectangular portion, but with differing construction methods. The burial chamber in the circular portion is 9.4 meters long by 3.4 meters wide and has walls made of large dressed blocked of [[andesite]], although the ceiling has collapsed. This is one of then largest burial chambers found in Gunma Prefecture. The one in the rectangular portion is lined with natural stones and is 8.76 meters long by 2.22 meters wide. From the stonework, it is estimated that this tumulus was built in the late 6th century AD, or towards the end of the Kofun period.<ref name="Shiseki">{{cite book |last1=Isomura |first1=Yukio |last2=Sakai |first2=Hideya |title=(国指定史跡事典) National Historic Site Encyclopedia |date=2012 |publisher=学生社 |isbn=4311750404}}{{in lang|ja}}</ref>
The tomb was opened in then mid-[[Edo period]], and many [[grave goods]] including bronze and iron swords, ''[[magatama]]'' beads, gold earrings, long-necked iron jars, and [[Sue ware]] earthenware pottery were recovered. A few of these artifacts are preserved at the [[Tokyo National Museum]], but most have been lost over the years. These include at least one sword with a silver hilt, which is mentioned in the Edo period records, but whose whereabouts are unknown.
In 1874, in response to a [[Meiji government]] project to find the grave of the legendary Toyoki-irihiko no Mikoto, the founder of [[Keno Province]] mentioned in the [[Nara period]] ''[[Nihon Shoki]]'' chronicle, Gunma Prefecture officially submitted the Sōja Futagoyama Kofun as a possible candidate. This petition was denied by the [[Imperial Household Agency]] due to lack of evidence, and due to publicity received by findings at the [[Maefutago Kofun]] which was believed at the time to have a better claim.
;Overall length: 89.9 meters ;Posterior circular portion: 44 meter diameter x 7.5 meters high, 2-tier ;Anterior rectangular portion: 61 meters wide x 8 meters high, 2-tier
==See also== *[[List of Historic Sites of Japan (Gunma)]]
==References== {{reflist}}
==External links== *[https://www.maebashi-cvb.com/spot/1029 Maebashi City official guide] {{in lang|ja}}

==Overview== Sōja Futagoyama Kofun is a {{nihongo|''[[zenpō-kōen-fun]]''|前方後円墳}}, which is shaped like a keyhole, having one square end and one circular end, when viewed from above. The tumulus is located on a plain near the southeastern slopes of [[Mount Haruna]], and is one of a cluster of ''kofun'' tumuli in the area. It has a total length of 89.9 meters and was built in two tiers, orientated to the west. The surface was originally covered in ''[[fukiishi]]'' with rows of cylindrical ''[[haniwa]]''. From the topography of the site, it is believed that the tumulus was surrounded by a moat, although this has not yet been confirmed via [[archaeological excavation]]. Although the detailed structure of the mound has not been investigated yet, it is known to have two lateral [[burial chamber]]s, one in the posterior circular portion and one in the anterior rectangular portion, but with differing construction methods. The burial chamber in the circular portion is 9.4 meters long by 3.4 meters wide and has walls made of large dressed blocked of [[andesite]], although the ceiling has collapsed. This is one of then largest burial chambers found in Gunma Prefecture. The one in the rectangular portion is lined with natural stones and is 8.76 meters long by 2.22 meters wide. From the stonework, it is estimated that this tumulus was built in the late 6th century AD, or towards the end of the Kofun period.<ref name="Shiseki">{{cite book |last1=Isomura |first1=Yukio |last2=Sakai |first2=Hideya |title=(国指定史跡事典) National Historic Site Encyclopedia |date=2012 |publisher=学生社 |isbn=4311750404}}{{in lang|ja}}</ref>
The tomb was opened in then mid-[[Edo period]], and many [[grave goods]] including bronze and iron swords, ''[[magatama]]'' beads, gold earrings, long-necked iron jars, and [[Sue ware]] earthenware pottery were recovered. A few of these artifacts are preserved at the [[Tokyo National Museum]], but most have been lost over the years. These include at least one sword with a silver hilt, which is mentioned in the Edo period records, but whose whereabouts are unknown.
In 1874, in response to a [[Meiji government]] project to find the grave of the legendary Toyoki-irihiko no Mikoto, the founder of [[Keno Province]] mentioned in the [[Nara period]] ''[[Nihon Shoki]]'' chronicle, Gunma Prefecture officially submitted the Sōja Futagoyama Kofun as a possible candidate. This petition was denied by the [[Imperial Household Agency]] due to lack of evidence, and due to publicity received by findings at the [[Maefutago Kofun]] which was believed at the time to have a better claim.
;Overall length: 89.9 meters ;Posterior circular portion: 44 meter diameter x 7.5 meters high, 2-tier ;Anterior rectangular portion: 61 meters wide x 8 meters high, 2-tier
==See also== *[[List of Historic Sites of Japan (Gunma)]]
==References== {{reflist}}
==External links== *[https://www.maebashi-cvb.com/spot/1029 Maebashi City official guide] {{in lang|ja}}

{{Short description|Burial mound in Japan}} {{Infobox ancient site | name =Sōja Futagoyama Kofun | native_name =総社二子山古墳 | native_name_lang = | image = Maebashi Souzya-hutagoyama Kohun 1.jpg | image_size = | alt = | caption = Sōja Futagoyama Kofun | map_type = Japan Gunma Prefecture#Japan | map_alt = Location in Japan | relief = 1 | map_caption = Sōja Futagoyama Kofun | map_size = 270px | altitude_m = | altitude_ref = | coordinates = {{coord|36|24|41|N|139|2|5.3|E|region:JP|display=inline}} | map_dot_label = | location = [[Maebashi, Gunma]], [[Japan]] | region = [[Kantō region]] | type = [[kofun]] | part_of = | length = | width = | area = | volume = | diameter = | circumference = | height = | built =late 6th century | abandoned = | epochs =[[Kofun period]] | cultures = | discovered = | excavations = | archaeologists = | condition = | ownership = | management = | public_access = Yes (Park) | other_designation = | website = <!-- {{URL|example.com}} --> | architectural_styles = | architectural_details = |notes = {{box|background=white|align=center|wide=yes|border size=3px|border color=brown|text align=center|[[Monuments of Japan|National Historic Site of Japan]]}} }}
{{nihongo|'''Sōja Futagoyama Kofun'''|総社二子山古墳|}} is a [[Kofun period]] [[kofun|burial mound]] located in what is now the city of [[Maebashi, Gunma|Maebashi]], [[Gunma Prefecture]] in the northern [[Kantō region]] of [[Japan]]. It was designated a [[Monuments of Japan|National Historic Site of Japan]] in 1927.<ref name="Bunka1">{{cite web |url=https://bunka.nii.ac.jp/heritages/detail/171625|title=二子山古墳|language=ja |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] }}</ref> It is also sometimes referred to as the {{nihongo|'''Dantaiyama Kofun'''|男体山古墳|}}.
==Overview== Sōja Futagoyama Kofun is a {{nihongo|''[[zenpō-kōen-fun]]''|前方後円墳}}, which is shaped like a keyhole, having one square end and one circular end, when viewed from above. The tumulus is located on a plain near the southeastern slopes of [[Mount Haruna]], and is one of a cluster of ''kofun'' tumuli in the area. It has a total length of 89.9 meters and was built in two tiers, orientated to the west. The surface was originally covered in ''[[fukiishi]]'' with rows of cylindrical ''[[haniwa]]''. From the topography of the site, it is believed that the tumulus was surrounded by a moat, although this has not yet been confirmed via [[archaeological excavation]]. Although the detailed structure of the mound has not been investigated yet, it is known to have two lateral [[burial chamber]]s, one in the posterior circular portion and one in the anterior rectangular portion, but with differing construction methods. The burial chamber in the circular portion is 9.4 meters long by 3.4 meters wide and has walls made of large dressed blocked of [[andesite]], although the ceiling has collapsed. This is one of then largest burial chambers found in Gunma Prefecture. The one in the rectangular portion is lined with natural stones and is 8.76 meters long by 2.22 meters wide. From the stonework, it is estimated that this tumulus was built in the late 6th century AD, or towards the end of the Kofun period.<ref name="Shiseki">{{cite book |last1=Isomura |first1=Yukio |last2=Sakai |first2=Hideya |title=(国指定史跡事典) National Historic Site Encyclopedia |date=2012 |publisher=学生社 |isbn=4311750404}}{{in lang|ja}}</ref>
The tomb was opened in then mid-[[Edo period]], and many [[grave goods]] including bronze and iron swords, ''[[magatama]]'' beads, gold earrings, long-necked iron jars, and [[Sue ware]] earthenware pottery were recovered. A few of these artifacts are preserved at the [[Tokyo National Museum]], but most have been lost over the years. These include at least one sword with a silver hilt, which is mentioned in the Edo period records, but whose whereabouts are unknown.
In 1874, in response to a [[Meiji government]] project to find the grave of the legendary Toyoki-irihiko no Mikoto, the founder of [[Keno Province]] mentioned in the [[Nara period]] ''[[Nihon Shoki]]'' chronicle, Gunma Prefecture officially submitted the Sōja Futagoyama Kofun as a possible candidate. This petition was denied by the [[Imperial Household Agency]] due to lack of evidence, and due to publicity received by findings at the [[Maefutago Kofun]] which was believed at the time to have a better claim.
;Overall length: 89.9 meters ;Posterior circular portion: 44 meter diameter x 7.5 meters high, 2-tier ;Anterior rectangular portion: 61 meters wide x 8 meters high, 2-tier

{{Short description|Burial mound in Japan}} {{Infobox ancient site | name =Sōja Futagoyama Kofun | native_name =総社二子山古墳 | native_name_lang = | image = Maebashi Souzya-hutagoyama Kohun 1.jpg | image_size = | alt = | caption = Sōja Futagoyama Kofun | map_type = Japan Gunma Prefecture#Japan | map_alt = location in Japan | relief = 1 | map_caption = Sōja Futagoyama Kofun | map_size = 270px | altitude_m = | altitude_ref = | coordinates = {{coord|36|24|41|N|139|2|5.3|E|region:JP|display=inline}} | map_dot_label = | location = [[Maebashi, Gunma]], [[Japan]] | region = [[Kantō region]] | type = [[kofun]] | part_of = | length = | width = | area = | volume = | diameter = | circumference = | height = | built =late 6th century | abandoned = | epochs =[[Kofun period]] | cultures = | discovered = | excavations = | archaeologists = | condition = | ownership = | management = | public_access = Yes (Park) | other_designation = | website = <!-- {{URL|example.com}} --> | architectural_styles = | architectural_details = |notes = {{box|background=white|align=center|wide=yes|border size=3px|border color=brown|text align=center|[[Monuments of Japan|National Historic Site of Japan]]}} }}
{{nihongo|'''Sōja Futagoyama Kofun'''|総社二子山古墳|}} is a [[Kofun period]] [[kofun|burial mound]] located in what is now the city of [[Maebashi, Gunma|Maebashi]], [[Gunma Prefecture]] in the northern [[Kantō region]] of [[Japan]]. It was designated a [[Monuments of Japan|National Historic Site of Japan]] in 1927.<ref name="Bunka1">{{cite web |url=https://bunka.nii.ac.jp/heritages/detail/171625|title=二子山古墳|language=ja |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] }}</ref> It is also sometimes referred to as the {{nihongo|'''Dantaiyama Kofun'''|男体山古墳|}}.
==Overview== Sōja Futagoyama Kofun is a {{nihongo|''[[zenpō-kōen-fun]]''|前方後円墳}}, which is shaped like a keyhole, having one square end and one circular end, when viewed from above. The tumulus is located on a plain near the southeastern slopes of [[Mount Haruna]], and is one of a cluster of ''kofun'' tumuli in the area. It has a total length of 89.9 meters and was built in two tiers, orientated to the west. The surface was originally covered in ''[[fukiishi]]'' with rows of cylindrical ''[[haniwa]]''. From the topography of the site, it is believed that the tumulus was surrounded by a moat, although this has not yet been confirmed via [[archaeological excavation]]. Although the detailed structure of the mound has not been investigated yet, it is known to have two lateral [[burial chamber]]s, one in the posterior circular portion and one in the anterior rectangular portion, but with differing construction methods. The burial chamber in the circular portion is 9.4 meters long by 3.4 meters wide and has walls made of large dressed blocked of [[andesite]], although the ceiling has collapsed. This is one of then largest burial chambers found in Gunma Prefecture. The one in the rectangular portion is lined with natural stones and is 8.76 meters long by 2.22 meters wide. From the stonework, it is estimated that this tumulus was built in the late 6th century AD, or towards the end of the Kofun period.<ref name="Shiseki">{{cite book |last1=Isomura |first1=Yukio |last2=Sakai |first2=Hideya |title=(国指定史跡事典) National Historic Site Encyclopedia |date=2012 |publisher=学生社 |isbn=4311750404}}{{in lang|ja}}</ref>
The tomb was opened in then mid-[[Edo period]], and many [[grave goods]] including bronze and iron swords, ''[[magatama]]'' beads, gold earrings, long-necked iron jars, and [[Sue ware]] earthenware pottery were recovered. A few of these artifacts are preserved at the [[Tokyo National Museum]], but most have been lost over the years. These include at least one sword with a silver hilt, which is mentioned in the Edo period records, but whose whereabouts are unknown.
In 1874, in response to a [[Meiji government]] project to find the grave of the legendary Toyoki-irihiko no Mikoto, the founder of [[Keno Province]] mentioned in the [[Nara period]] ''[[Nihon Shoki]]'' chronicle, Gunma Prefecture officially submitted the Sōja Futagoyama Kofun as a possible candidate. This petition was denied by the [[Imperial Household Agency]] due to lack of evidence, and due to publicity received by findings at the [[Maefutago Kofun]] which was believed at the time to have a better claim.
;Overall length: 89.9 meters ;Posterior circular portion: 44 meter diameter x 7.5 meters high, 2-tier ;Anterior rectangular portion: 61 meters wide x 8 meters high, 2-tier
==See also== *[[List of Historic Sites of Japan (Gunma)]]
==References== {{reflist}}
==External links== *[https://www.maebashi-cvb.com/spot/1029 Maebashi City official guide] {{in lang|ja}}

{{Short description|Burial mound in Japan}} {{Infobox ancient site | name =Sōja Futagoyama Kofun | native_name =総社二子山古墳 | native_name_lang = | image = Maebashi Souzya-hutagoyama Kohun 1.jpg | image_size = | alt = | caption = Sōja Futagoyama Kofun | map_type = Japan Gunma Prefecture#Japan | map_alt = location in Japan | relief = 1 | map_caption = Sōja Futagoyama Kofun | map_size = 270px | altitude_m = | altitude_ref = | coordinates = {{coord|36|24|41|N|139|2|5.3|E|region:JP|display=inline}} | map_dot_label = | location = [[Maebashi, Gunma]], [[Japan]] | region = [[Kantō region]] | type = [[kofun]] | part_of = | length = | width = | area = | volume = | diameter = | circumference = | height = | built =late 6th century | abandoned = | epochs =[[Kofun period]] | cultures = | discovered = | excavations = | archaeologists = | condition = | ownership = | management = | public_access = Yes (Park) | other_designation = | website = <!-- {{URL|example.com}} --> | architectural_styles = | architectural_details = |notes = {{box|background=white|align=center|wide=yes|border size=3px|border color=brown|text align=center|[[Monuments of Japan|National Historic Site of Japan]]}} }}
{{nihongo|'''Sōja Futagoyama Kofun'''|総社二子山古墳|}} is a [[Kofun period]] [[kofun|burial mound]] located in what is now the city of [[Maebashi, Gunma|Maebashi]], [[Gunma Prefecture]] in the northern [[Kantō region]] of [[Japan]]. It was designated a [[Monuments of Japan|National Historic Site of Japan]] in 1927.<ref name="Bunka1">{{cite web |url=https://bunka.nii.ac.jp/heritages/detail/171625|title=二子山古墳|language=ja |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] }}</ref> It is also sometimes referred to as the {{nihongo|'''Dantaiyama Kofun'''|男体山古墳|}}.
==Overview== Sōja Futagoyama Kofun is a {{nihongo|''[[zenpō-kōen-fun]]''|前方後円墳}}, which is shaped like a keyhole, having one square end and one circular end, when viewed from above. The tumulus is located on a plain near the southeastern slopes of [[Mount Haruna]], and is one of a cluster of ''kofun'' tumuli in the area. It has a total length of 89.9 meters and was built in two tiers, orientated to the west. The surface was originally covered in ''[[fukiishi]]'' with rows of cylindrical ''[[haniwa]]''. From the topography of the site, it is believed that the tumulus was surrounded by a moat, although this has not yet been confirmed via [[archaeological excavation]]. Although the detailed structure of the mound has not been investigated yet, it is known to have two lateral [[burial chamber]]s, one in the posterior circular portion and one in the anterior rectangular portion, but with differing construction methods. The burial chamber in the circular portion is 9.4 meters long by 3.4 meters wide and has walls made of large dressed blocked of [[andesite]], although the ceiling has collapsed. This is one of then largest burial chambers found in Gunma Prefecture. The one in the rectangular portion is lined with natural stones and is 8.76 meters long by 2.22 meters wide. From the stonework, it is estimated that this tumulus was built in the late 6th century AD, or towards the end of the Kofun period.<ref name="Shiseki">{{cite book |last1=Isomura |first1=Yukio |last2=Sakai |first2=Hideya |title=(国指定史跡事典) National Historic Site Encyclopedia |date=2012 |publisher=学生社 |isbn=4311750404}}{{in lang|ja}}</ref>
The tomb was opened in then mid-[[Edo period]], and many [[grave goods]] including bronze and iron swords, ''[[magatama]]'' beads, gold earrings, long-necked iron jars, and [[Sue ware]] earthenware pottery were recovered. A few of these artifacts are preserved at the [[Tokyo National Museum]], but most have been lost over the years. These include at least one sword with a silver hilt, which is mentioned in the Edo period records, but whose whereabouts are unknown.
In 1874, in response to a [[Meiji government]] project to find the grave of the legendary Toyoki-irihiko no Mikoto, the founder of [[Keno Province]] mentioned in the [[Nara period]] ''[[Nihon Shoki]]'' chronicle, Gunma Prefecture officially submitted the Sōja Futagoyama Kofun as a possible candidate. This petition was denied by the [[Imperial Household Agency]] due to lack of evidence, and due to publicity received by findings at the [[Maefutago Kofun]] which was believed at the time to have a better claim.
;Overall length: 89.9 meters ;Posterior circular portion: 44 meter diameter x 7.5 meters high, 2-tier ;Anterior rectangular portion: 61 meters wide x 8 meters high, 2-tier
==See also== *[[List of Historic Sites of Japan (Gunma)]]
==References== {{reflist}}
==External links== *[https://www.maebashi-cvb.com/spot/1029 Maebashi City official guide] {{in lang|ja}}

revision.text:
{{Short description|Burial mound in Japan}} {{Infobox ancient site | name =Sōja Futagoyama Kofun | native_name =総社二子山古墳 | native_name_lang = | image = Maebashi Souzya-hutagoyama Kohun 1.jpg | image_size = | alt = | caption = Sōja Futagoyama Kofun | map_type = Japan Gunma Prefecture#Japan | map_alt = location in Japan | relief = 1 | map_caption = Sōja Futagoyama Kofun | map_size = 270px | altitude_m = | altitude_ref = | coordinates = {{coord|36|24|41|N|139|2|5.3|E|region:JP|display=inline}} | map_dot_label = | location = [[Maebashi, Gunma]], [[Japan]] | region = [[Kantō region]] | type = [[kofun]] | part_of = | length = | width = | area = | volume = | diameter = | circumference = | height = | built =late 6th century | abandoned = | epochs =[[Kofun period]] | cultures = | discovered = | excavations = | archaeologists = | condition = | ownership = | management = | public_access = Yes (Park) | other_designation = | website = <!-- {{URL|example.com}} --> | architectural_styles = | architectural_details = |notes = {{box|background=white|align=center|wide=yes|border size=3px|border color=brown|text align=center|[[Monuments of Japan|National Historic Site of Japan]]}} }}
{{nihongo|'''Sōja Futagoyama Kofun'''|総社二子山古墳|}} is a [[Kofun period]] [[kofun|burial mound]] located in what is now the city of [[Maebashi, Gunma|Maebashi]], [[Gunma Prefecture]] in the northern [[Kantō region]] of [[Japan]]. It was designated a [[Monuments of Japan|National Historic Site of Japan]] in 1927.<ref name="Bunka1">{{cite web |url=https://bunka.nii.ac.jp/heritages/detail/171625|title=二子山古墳|language=ja |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] }}</ref> It is also sometimes referred to as the {{nihongo|'''Dantaiyama Kofun'''|男体山古墳|}}.
==Overview== Sōja Futagoyama Kofun is a {{nihongo|''[[zenpō-kōen-fun]]''|前方後円墳}}, which is shaped like a keyhole, having one square end and one circular end, when viewed from above. The tumulus is located on a plain near the southeastern slopes of [[Mount Haruna]], and is one of a cluster of ''kofun'' tumuli in the area. It has a total length of 89.9 meters and was built in two tiers, orientated to the west. The surface was originally covered in ''[[fukiishi]]'' with rows of cylindrical ''[[haniwa]]''. From the topography of the site, it is believed that the tumulus was surrounded by a moat, although this has not yet been confirmed via [[archaeological excavation]]. Although the detailed structure of the mound has not been investigated yet, it is known to have two lateral [[burial chamber]]s, one in the posterior circular portion and one in the anterior rectangular portion, but with differing construction methods. The burial chamber in the circular portion is 9.4 meters long by 3.4 meters wide and has walls made of large dressed blocked of [[andesite]], although the ceiling has collapsed. This is one of then largest burial chambers found in Gunma Prefecture. The one in the rectangular portion is lined with natural stones and is 8.76 meters long by 2.22 meters wide. From the stonework, it is estimated that this tumulus was built in the late 6th century AD, or towards the end of the Kofun period.<ref name="Shiseki">{{cite book |last1=Isomura |first1=Yukio |last2=Sakai |first2=Hideya |title=(国指定史跡事典) National Historic Site Encyclopedia |date=2012 |publisher=学生社 |isbn=4311750404}}{{in lang|ja}}</ref>
The tomb was opened in then mid-[[Edo period]], and many [[grave goods]] including bronze and iron swords, ''[[magatama]]'' beads, gold earrings, long-necked iron jars, and [[Sue ware]] earthenware pottery were recovered. A few of these artifacts are preserved at the [[Tokyo National Museum]], but most have been lost over the years. These include at least one sword with a silver hilt, which is mentioned in the Edo period records, but whose whereabouts are unknown.
In 1874, in response to a [[Meiji government]] project to find the grave of the legendary Toyoki-irihiko no Mikoto, the founder of [[Keno Province]] mentioned in the [[Nara period]] ''[[Nihon Shoki]]'' chronicle, Gunma Prefecture officially submitted the Sōja Futagoyama Kofun as a possible candidate. This petition was denied by the [[Imperial Household Agency]] due to lack of evidence, and due to publicity received by findings at the [[Maefutago Kofun]] which was believed at the time to have a better claim.
;Overall length: 89.9 meters ;Posterior circular portion: 44 meter diameter x 7.5 meters high, 2-tier ;Anterior rectangular portion: 61 meters wide x 8 meters high, 2-tier
==See also== *[[List of Historic Sites of Japan (Gunma)]]
==References== {{reflist}}
==External links== *[https://www.maebashi-cvb.com/spot/1029 Maebashi City official guide] {{in lang|ja}}

{{nihongo|'''Sōja Futagoyama Kofun'''|総社二子山古墳|}} is a [[Kofun period]] [[kofun|burial mound]] located in what is now the city of [[Maebashi, Gunma|Maebashi]], [[Gunma Prefecture]] in the northern [[Kantō region]] of [[Japan]]. It was designated a [[Monuments of Japan|National Historic Site of Japan]] in 1927.<ref name="Bunka1">{{cite web |url=https://bunka.nii.ac.jp/heritages/detail/171625|title=二子山古墳|language=ja |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] }}</ref> It is also sometimes referred to as the {{nihongo|'''Dantaiyama Kofun'''|男体山古墳|}}.

The tumulus is located on a plain near the southeastern slopes of [[Mount Haruna]], and is one of a cluster of ''kofun'' tumuli in the area. The burial chamber in the circular portion is 9.4 meters long by 3.4 meters wide and has walls made of large dressed blocked of [[andesite]], although the ceiling has collapsed. This is one of then largest burial chambers found in Gunma Prefecture. The one in the rectangular portion is lined with natural stones and is 8.76 meters long by 2.22 meters wide. From the stonework, it is estimated that this tumulus was built in the late 6th century AD, or towards the end of the Kofun period.<ref name="Shiseki">{{cite book |last1=Isomura |first1=Yukio |last2=Sakai |first2=Hideya |title=(国指定史跡事典) National Historic Site Encyclopedia |date=2012 |publisher=学生社 |isbn=4311750404}}{{in lang|ja}}</ref>

A few of these artifacts are preserved at the [[Tokyo National Museum]], but most have been lost over the years. revision.text:
{{Short description|Burial mound in Japan}} {{Infobox ancient site | name =Sōja Futagoyama Kofun | native_name =総社二子山古墳 | native_name_lang = | image = Maebashi Souzya-hutagoyama Kohun 1.jpg | image_size = | alt = | caption = Sōja Futagoyama Kofun | map_type = Japan Gunma Prefecture#Japan | map_alt = location in Japan | relief = 1 | map_caption = Sōja Futagoyama Kofun | map_size = 270px | altitude_m = | altitude_ref = | coordinates = {{coord|36|24|41|N|139|2|5.3|E|region:JP|display=inline}} | map_dot_label = | location = [[Maebashi, Gunma]], [[Japan]] | region = [[Kantō region]] | type = [[kofun]] | part_of = | length = | width = | area = | volume = | diameter = | circumference = | height = | built =late 6th century | abandoned = | epochs =[[Kofun period]] | cultures = | discovered = | excavations = | archaeologists = | condition = | ownership = | management = | public_access = Yes (Park) | other_designation = | website = <!-- {{URL|example.com}} --> | architectural_styles = | architectural_details = |notes = {{box|background=white|align=center|wide=yes|border size=3px|border color=brown|text align=center|[[Monuments of Japan|National Historic Site of Japan]]}} }}
{{nihongo|'''Sōja Futagoyama Kofun'''|総社二子山古墳|}} is a [[Kofun period]] [[kofun|burial mound]] located in what is now the city of [[Maebashi, Gunma|Maebashi]], [[Gunma Prefecture]] in the northern [[Kantō region]] of [[Japan]]. It was designated a [[Monuments of Japan|National Historic Site of Japan]] in 1927.<ref name="Bunka1">{{cite web |url=https://bunka.nii.ac.jp/heritages/detail/171625|title=二子山古墳|language=ja |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] }}</ref> It is also sometimes referred to as the {{nihongo|'''Dantaiyama Kofun'''|男体山古墳|}}.
==Overview== Sōja Futagoyama Kofun is a {{nihongo|''[[zenpō-kōen-fun]]''|前方後円墳}}, which is shaped like a keyhole, having one square end and one circular end, when viewed from above. The tumulus is located on a plain near the southeastern slopes of [[Mount Haruna]], and is one of a cluster of ''kofun'' tumuli in the area. It has a total length of 89.9 meters and was built in two tiers, orientated to the west. The surface was originally covered in ''[[fukiishi]]'' with rows of cylindrical ''[[haniwa]]''. From the topography of the site, it is believed that the tumulus was surrounded by a moat, although this has not yet been confirmed via [[archaeological excavation]]. Although the detailed structure of the mound has not been investigated yet, it is known to have two lateral [[burial chamber]]s, one in the posterior circular portion and one in the anterior rectangular portion, but with differing construction methods. The burial chamber in the circular portion is 9.4 meters long by 3.4 meters wide and has walls made of large dressed blocked of [[andesite]], although the ceiling has collapsed. This is one of then largest burial chambers found in Gunma Prefecture. The one in the rectangular portion is lined with natural stones and is 8.76 meters long by 2.22 meters wide. From the stonework, it is estimated that this tumulus was built in the late 6th century AD, or towards the end of the Kofun period.<ref name="Shiseki">{{cite book |last1=Isomura |first1=Yukio |last2=Sakai |first2=Hideya |title=(国指定史跡事典) National Historic Site Encyclopedia |date=2012 |publisher=学生社 |isbn=4311750404}}{{in lang|ja}}</ref>
The tomb was opened in then mid-[[Edo period]], and many [[grave goods]] including bronze and iron swords, ''[[magatama]]'' beads, gold earrings, long-necked iron jars, and [[Sue ware]] earthenware pottery were recovered. A few of these artifacts are preserved at the [[Tokyo National Museum]], but most have been lost over the years. These include at least one sword with a silver hilt, which is mentioned in the Edo period records, but whose whereabouts are unknown.
In 1874, in response to a [[Meiji government]] project to find the grave of the legendary Toyoki-irihiko no Mikoto, the founder of [[Keno Province]] mentioned in the [[Nara period]] ''[[Nihon Shoki]]'' chronicle, Gunma Prefecture officially submitted the Sōja Futagoyama Kofun as a possible candidate. This petition was denied by the [[Imperial Household Agency]] due to lack of evidence, and due to publicity received by findings at the [[Maefutago Kofun]] which was believed at the time to have a better claim.
;Overall length: 89.9 meters ;Posterior circular portion: 44 meter diameter x 7.5 meters high, 2-tier ;Anterior rectangular portion: 61 meters wide x 8 meters high, 2-tier

revision.text:
{{Short description|Burial mound in Japan}} {{Infobox ancient site | name =Sōja Futagoyama Kofun | native_name =総社二子山古墳 | native_name_lang = | image = Maebashi Souzya-hutagoyama Kohun 1.jpg | image_size = | alt = | caption = Sōja Futagoyama Kofun | map_type = Japan Gunma Prefecture#Japan | map_alt = location in Japan | relief = 1 | map_caption = Sōja Futagoyama Kofun | map_size = 270px | altitude_m = | altitude_ref = | coordinates = {{coord|36|24|41|N|139|2|5.3|E|region:JP|display=inline}} | map_dot_label = | location = [[Maebashi, Gunma]], [[Japan]] | region = [[Kantō region]] | type = [[kofun]] | part_of = | length = | width = | area = | volume = | diameter = | circumference = | height = | built =late 6th century | abandoned = | epochs =[[Kofun period]] | cultures = | discovered = | excavations = | archaeologists = | condition = | ownership = | management = | public_access = Yes (Park) | other_designation = | website = <!-- {{URL|example.com}} --> | architectural_styles = | architectural_details = |notes = {{box|background=white|align=center|wide=yes|border size=3px|border color=brown|text align=center|[[Monuments of Japan|National Historic Site of Japan]]}} }}
{{nihongo|'''Sōja Futagoyama Kofun'''|総社二子山古墳|}} is a [[Kofun period]] [[kofun|burial mound]] located in what is now the city of [[Maebashi, Gunma|Maebashi]], [[Gunma Prefecture]] in the northern [[Kantō region]] of [[Japan]]. It was designated a [[Monuments of Japan|National Historic Site of Japan]] in 1927.<ref name="Bunka1">{{cite web |url=https://bunka.nii.ac.jp/heritages/detail/171625|title=二子山古墳|language=ja |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] }}</ref> It is also sometimes referred to as the {{nihongo|'''Dantaiyama Kofun'''|男体山古墳|}}.
==Overview== Sōja Futagoyama Kofun is a {{nihongo|''[[zenpō-kōen-fun]]''|前方後円墳}}, which is shaped like a keyhole, having one square end and one circular end, when viewed from above. The tumulus is located on a plain near the southeastern slopes of [[Mount Haruna]], and is one of a cluster of ''kofun'' tumuli in the area. It has a total length of 89.9 meters and was built in two tiers, orientated to the west. The surface was originally covered in ''[[fukiishi]]'' with rows of cylindrical ''[[haniwa]]''. From the topography of the site, it is believed that the tumulus was surrounded by a moat, although this has not yet been confirmed via [[archaeological excavation]]. Although the detailed structure of the mound has not been investigated yet, it is known to have two lateral [[burial chamber]]s, one in the posterior circular portion and one in the anterior rectangular portion, but with differing construction methods. The burial chamber in the circular portion is 9.4 meters long by 3.4 meters wide and has walls made of large dressed blocked of [[andesite]], although the ceiling has collapsed. This is one of then largest burial chambers found in Gunma Prefecture. The one in the rectangular portion is lined with natural stones and is 8.76 meters long by 2.22 meters wide. From the stonework, it is estimated that this tumulus was built in the late 6th century AD, or towards the end of the Kofun period.<ref name="Shiseki">{{cite book |last1=Isomura |first1=Yukio |last2=Sakai |first2=Hideya |title=(国指定史跡事典) National Historic Site Encyclopedia |date=2012 |publisher=学生社 |isbn=4311750404}}{{in lang|ja}}</ref>
The tomb was opened in then mid-[[Edo period]], and many [[grave goods]] including bronze and iron swords, ''[[magatama]]'' beads, gold earrings, long-necked iron jars, and [[Sue ware]] earthenware pottery were recovered. A few of these artifacts are preserved at the [[Tokyo National Museum]], but most have been lost over the years. These include at least one sword with a silver hilt, which is mentioned in the Edo period records, but whose whereabouts are unknown.
In 1874, in response to a [[Meiji government]] project to find the grave of the legendary Toyoki-irihiko no Mikoto, the founder of [[Keno Province]] mentioned in the [[Nara period]] ''[[Nihon Shoki]]'' chronicle, Gunma Prefecture officially submitted the Sōja Futagoyama Kofun as a possible candidate. This petition was denied by the [[Imperial Household Agency]] due to lack of evidence, and due to publicity received by findings at the [[Maefutago Kofun]] which was believed at the time to have a better claim.
;Overall length: 89.9 meters ;Posterior circular portion: 44 meter diameter x 7.5 meters high, 2-tier ;Anterior rectangular portion: 61 meters wide x 8 meters high, 2-tier

revision.text:
{{Short description|Kofun in Japan}} {{Infobox ancient site|type = [[Zenpokoenfun]] | name =Sōja Futagoyama Kofun | native_name =総社二子山古墳 | native_name_lang = | image = Maebashi Souzya-hutagoyama Kohun 1.jpg | image_size = | alt = | caption = Sōja Futagoyama Kofun | map_type = Japan Gunma Prefecture#Japan | map_alt = location in Japan | relief = 1 | map_caption = Sōja Futagoyama Kofun | map_size = 270px | altitude_m = | altitude_ref = | coordinates = {{coord|36|24|41|N|139|2|5.3|E|region:JP|display=inline}} | map_dot_label = | location = [[Maebashi, Gunma]], [[Japan]] | region = [[Kantō region]] | type = [[kofun]] | part_of = | length = | width = | area = | volume = | diameter = | circumference = | height = | built =late 6th century | abandoned = | epochs =[[Kofun period]] | cultures = | discovered = | excavations = | archaeologists = | condition = | ownership = | management = | public_access = Yes (Park) | Historic Site = | website = <!-- {{URL|example.com}} --> | architectural_styles = | architectural_details = |notes = {{box|background=white|align=center|wide=yes|border size=3px|border color=brown|text align=center|[[Monuments of Japan|National Historic Site of Japan]]}} }}
{{nihongo|'''Sōja Futagoyama Kofun'''|総社二子山古墳|1=Sōja Futagoyama Kofun|}} is a [[Kofun period]] [[kofun|Kofun]] located in what is now the city of [[Maebashi, Gunma|Maebashi]], [[Gunma Prefecture]] in the northern [[Kantō region]] of [[Japan]]. It was designated a [[Monuments of Japan|National Historic Site of Japan]] in 1927.<ref name="Bunka1">{{cite web |url=https://bunka.nii.ac.jp/heritages/detail/171625|title=二子山古墳|language=ja |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] }}</ref> It is also sometimes referred to as the {{nihongo|'''Dantaiyama Kofun'''|男体山古墳|}}.
==Overview== Sōja Futagoyama Kofun is a {{nihongo|''[[zenpō-kōen-fun]]''|前方後円墳|1=Zenpokoenfun}}, which is shaped like a keyhole, having one square end and one circular end, when viewed from above. The tumulus is located on a plain near the southeastern slopes of [[Mount Haruna]], and is one of a cluster of ''kofun'' tumuli in the area. It has a total length of 89.9 meters and was built in two tiers, orientated to the west. The surface was originally covered in ''[[fukiishi]]'' with rows of cylindrical ''[[haniwa]]''. From the topography of the site, it is believed that the tumulus was surrounded by a moat, although this has not yet been confirmed via [[archaeological excavation]]. Although the detailed structure of the mound has not been investigated yet, it is known to have two lateral [[burial chamber]]s, one in the posterior circular portion and one in the anterior rectangular portion, but with differing construction methods. The burial chamber in the circular portion is 9.4 meters long by 3.4 meters wide and has walls made of large dressed blocked of [[andesite]], although the ceiling has collapsed. This is one of then largest burial chambers found in Gunma Prefecture. The one in the rectangular portion is lined with natural stones and is 8.76 meters long by 2.22 meters wide. From the stonework, it is estimated that this tumulus was built in the late 6th century AD, or towards the end of the Kofun period.<ref name="Shiseki">{{cite book |last1=Isomura |first1=Yukio |last2=Sakai |first2=Hideya |title=(国指定史跡事典) National Historic Site Encyclopedia |date=2012 |publisher=学生社 |isbn=4311750404}}{{in lang|ja}}</ref>
The tomb was opened in then mid-[[Edo period]], and many [[grave goods]] including bronze and iron swords, ''[[magatama]]'' beads, gold earrings, long-necked iron jars, and [[Sue pottery]] earthenware pottery were recovered. A few of these artifacts are preserved at the [[Tokyo National Museum]], but most have been lost over the years. These include at least one sword with a silver hilt, which is mentioned in the Edo period records, but whose whereabouts are unknown.
In 1874, in response to a [[Meiji government]] project to find the grave of the legendary Toyoki-irihiko no Mikoto, the founder of [[Keno Province]] mentioned in the [[Nara period]] ''[[Nihon Shoki]]'' chronicle, Gunma Prefecture officially submitted the Sōja Futagoyama Kofun as a possible candidate. This petition was denied by the [[Imperial Household Agency]] due to lack of evidence, and due to publicity received by findings at the [[Maefutago Kofun]] which was believed at the time to have a better claim.
;Overall length: 89.9 meters ;Posterior circular portion: 44 meter diameter x 7.5 meters high, 2-tier ;Anterior rectangular portion: 61 meters wide x 8 meters high, 2-tier
==See also== *[[List of Historic Sites of Japan (Gunma)]]
==References== {{reflist}}
==External links== *[https://www.maebashi-cvb.com/spot/1029 Maebashi City official guide] {{in lang|ja}}
Sōja Futagoyama Kofun

These include at least one sword with a silver hilt, which is mentioned in the Edo period records, but whose whereabouts are unknown.
In 1874, in response to a [[Meiji government]] project to find the grave of the legendary Toyoki-irihiko no Mikoto, the founder of [[Keno Province]] mentioned in the [[Nara period]] ''[[Nihon Shoki]]'' chronicle, Gunma Prefecture officially submitted the Sōja Futagoyama Kofun as a possible candidate. This petition was denied by the [[Imperial Household Agency]] due to lack of evidence, and due to publicity received by findings at the [[Maefutago Kofun]] which was believed at the time to have a better claim.
;Overall length: 89.9 meters ;Posterior circular portion: 44 meter diameter x 7.5 meters high, 2-tier ;Anterior rectangular portion: 61 meters wide x 8 meters high, 2-tier

{{Short description|Kofun in Japan}} {{Infobox ancient site|type = [[Zenpokoenfun]] | name =Sōja Futagoyama Kofun | native_name =総社二子山古墳 | native_name_lang = | image = Maebashi Souzya-hutagoyama Kohun 1.jpg | image_size = | alt = | caption = Sōja Futagoyama Kofun | map_type = Japan Gunma Prefecture#Japan | map_alt = location in Japan | relief = 1 | map_caption = Sōja Futagoyama Kofun | map_size = 270px | altitude_m = | altitude_ref = | coordinates = {{coord|36|24|41|N|139|2|5.3|E|region:JP|display=inline}} | map_dot_label = | location = [[Maebashi, Gunma]], [[Japan]] | region = [[Kantō region]] | type = [[kofun]] | part_of = | length = | width = | area = | volume = | diameter = | circumference = | height = | built =late 6th century | abandoned = | epochs =[[Kofun period]] | cultures = | discovered = | excavations = | archaeologists = | condition = | ownership = | management = | public_access = Yes (Park) | Historic Site = | website = <!-- {{URL|example.com}} --> | architectural_styles = | architectural_details = |notes = {{box|background=white|align=center|wide=yes|border size=3px|border color=brown|text align=center|[[Monuments of Japan|National Historic Site of Japan]]}} }}
{{nihongo|'''Sōja Futagoyama Kofun'''|総社二子山古墳|1=Sōja Futagoyama Kofun|}} is a [[Kofun period]] [[kofun|Kofun]] located in what is now the city of [[Maebashi, Gunma|Maebashi]], [[Gunma Prefecture]] in the northern [[Kantō region]] of [[Japan]]. It was designated a [[Monuments of Japan|National Historic Site of Japan]] in 1927.<ref name="Bunka1">{{cite web |url=https://bunka.nii.ac.jp/heritages/detail/171625|title=二子山古墳|language=ja |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] }}</ref> It is also sometimes referred to as the {{nihongo|'''Dantaiyama Kofun'''|男体山古墳|}}.
==Overview== Sōja Futagoyama Kofun is a {{nihongo|''[[zenpō-kōen-fun]]''|前方後円墳|1=Zenpokoenfun}}, which is shaped like a keyhole, having one square end and one circular end, when viewed from above. The tumulus is located on a plain near the southeastern slopes of [[Mount Haruna]], and is one of a cluster of ''kofun'' tumuli in the area. It has a total length of 89.9 meters and was built in two tiers, orientated to the west. The surface was originally covered in ''[[fukiishi]]'' with rows of cylindrical ''[[haniwa]]''. From the topography of the site, it is believed that the tumulus was surrounded by a moat, although this has not yet been confirmed via [[archaeological excavation]]. Although the detailed structure of the mound has not been investigated yet, it is known to have two lateral [[burial chamber]]s, one in the posterior circular portion and one in the anterior rectangular portion, but with differing construction methods. The burial chamber in the circular portion is 9.4 meters long by 3.4 meters wide and has walls made of large dressed blocked of [[andesite]], although the ceiling has collapsed. This is one of then largest burial chambers found in Gunma Prefecture. The one in the rectangular portion is lined with natural stones and is 8.76 meters long by 2.22 meters wide. From the stonework, it is estimated that this tumulus was built in the late 6th century AD, or towards the end of the Kofun period.<ref name="Shiseki">{{cite book |last1=Isomura |first1=Yukio |last2=Sakai |first2=Hideya |title=(国指定史跡事典) National Historic Site Encyclopedia |date=2012 |publisher=学生社 |isbn=4311750404}}{{in lang|ja}}</ref>
The tomb was opened in then mid-[[Edo period]], and many [[grave goods]] including bronze and iron swords, ''[[magatama]]'' beads, gold earrings, long-necked iron jars, and [[Sue pottery]] earthenware pottery were recovered. A few of these artifacts are preserved at the [[Tokyo National Museum]], but most have been lost over the years. These include at least one sword with a silver hilt, which is mentioned in the Edo period records, but whose whereabouts are unknown.
In 1874, in response to a [[Meiji government]] project to find the grave of the legendary Toyoki-irihiko no Mikoto, the founder of [[Keno Province]] mentioned in the [[Nara period]] ''[[Nihon Shoki]]'' chronicle, Gunma Prefecture officially submitted the Sōja Futagoyama Kofun as a possible candidate. This petition was denied by the [[Imperial Household Agency]] due to lack of evidence, and due to publicity received by findings at the [[Maefutago Kofun]] which was believed at the time to have a better claim.
;Overall length: 89.9 meters ;Posterior circular portion: 44 meter diameter x 7.5 meters high, 2-tier ;Anterior rectangular portion: 61 meters wide x 8 meters high, 2-tier

The tomb was opened in then mid-[[Edo period]], and many [[grave goods]] including bronze and iron swords, ''[[magatama]]'' beads, gold earrings, long-necked iron jars, and [[Sue pottery]] earthenware pottery were recovered. A few of these artifacts are preserved at the [[Tokyo National Museum]], but most have been lost over the years. These include at least one sword with a silver hilt, which is mentioned in the Edo period records, but whose whereabouts are unknown.
In 1874, in response to a [[Meiji government]] project to find the grave of the legendary Toyoki-irihiko no Mikoto, the founder of [[Keno Province]] mentioned in the [[Nara period]] ''[[Nihon Shoki]]'' chronicle, Gunma Prefecture officially submitted the Sōja Futagoyama Kofun as a possible candidate. This petition was denied by the [[Imperial Household Agency]] due to lack of evidence, and due to publicity received by findings at the [[Mae Futago Kofun|Maefutago Kofun]] which was believed at the time to have a better claim.
;Overall length: 89.9 meters ;Posterior circular portion: 44 meter diameter x 7.5 meters high, 2-tier ;Anterior rectangular portion: 61 meters wide x 8 meters high, 2-tier

{{nihongo|'''Sōja Futagoyama Kofun'''|総社二子山古墳|1=Sōja Futagoyama Kofun|}} is a [[Kofun period]] [[kofun|Kofun]] located in what is now the city of [[Maebashi, Gunma|Maebashi]], [[Gunma Prefecture]] in the northern [[Kantō region]] of [[Japan]]. It was designated a [[Monuments of Japan|National Historic Site of Japan]] in 1927.<ref name="Bunka1">{{cite web |url=https://bunka.nii.ac.jp/heritages/detail/171625|title=二子山古墳|language=ja |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] }}</ref> It is also sometimes referred to as the {{nihongo|'''Dantaiyama Kofun'''|男体山古墳|}}.
==Overview== Sōja Futagoyama Kofun is a {{nihongo|''[[zenpō-kōen-fun]]''|前方後円墳|lt=Zenpokoenfun|1=Zenpokoenfun|comment=doing this because I need to differentiate it in piped links too with my new interwiki tool as not the ambiguous phrase keyhole shaped kofun|1=Zenpokoenfun|1=Zenpokoenfun}}, which is shaped like a keyhole, having one square end and one circular end, when viewed from above. The tumulus is located on a plain near the southeastern slopes of [[Mount Haruna]], and is one of a cluster of ''kofun'' tumuli in the area. It has a total length of 89.9 meters and was built in two tiers, orientated to the west. The surface was originally covered in ''[[fukiishi]]'' with rows of cylindrical ''[[haniwa]]''. From the topography of the site, it is believed that the tumulus was surrounded by a moat, although this has not yet been confirmed via [[archaeological excavation]]. Although the detailed structure of the mound has not been investigated yet, it is known to have two lateral [[burial chamber]]s, one in the posterior circular portion and one in the anterior rectangular portion, but with differing construction methods. The burial chamber in the circular portion is 9.4 meters long by 3.4 meters wide and has walls made of large dressed blocked of [[andesite]], although the ceiling has collapsed. This is one of then largest burial chambers found in Gunma Prefecture. The one in the rectangular portion is lined with natural stones and is 8.76 meters long by 2.22 meters wide. From the stonework, it is estimated that this tumulus was built in the late 6th century AD, or towards the end of the Kofun period.<ref name="Shiseki">{{cite book |last1=Isomura |first1=Yukio |last2=Sakai |first2=Hideya |title=(国指定史跡事典) National Historic Site Encyclopedia |date=2012 |publisher=学生社 |isbn=4311750404}}{{in lang|ja}}</ref>
The tomb was opened in then mid-[[Edo period]], and many [[grave goods]] including bronze and iron swords, ''[[magatama]]'' beads, gold earrings, long-necked iron jars, and [[Sue pottery]] earthenware pottery were recovered. A few of these artifacts are preserved at the [[Tokyo National Museum]], but most have been lost over the years. These include at least one sword with a silver hilt, which is mentioned in the Edo period records, but whose whereabouts are unknown.
In 1874, in response to a [[Meiji government]] project to find the grave of the legendary Toyoki-irihiko no Mikoto, the founder of [[Keno Province]] mentioned in the [[Nara period]] ''[[Nihon Shoki]]'' chronicle, Gunma Prefecture officially submitted the Sōja Futagoyama Kofun as a possible candidate. This petition was denied by the [[Imperial Household Agency]] due to lack of evidence, and due to publicity received by findings at the [[Mae Futago Kofun|Maefutago Kofun]] which was believed at the time to have a better claim.
;Overall length: 89.9 meters ;Posterior circular portion: 44 meter diameter x 7.5 meters high, 2-tier ;Anterior rectangular portion: 61 meters wide x 8 meters high, 2-tier
==See also== *[[List of Historic Sites of Japan (Gunma)]]
==References== {{reflist}}
==External links== *[https://www.maebashi-cvb.com/spot/1029 Maebashi City official guide] {{in lang|ja}}

These include at least one sword with a silver hilt, which is mentioned in the Edo period records, but whose whereabouts are unknown.
In 1874, in response to a [[Meiji government]] project to find the grave of the legendary Toyoki-irihiko no Mikoto, the founder of [[Keno Province]] mentioned in the [[Nara period]] ''[[Nihon Shoki]]'' chronicle, Gunma Prefecture officially submitted the Sōja Futagoyama Kofun as a possible candidate. This petition was denied by the [[Imperial Household Agency]] due to lack of evidence, and due to publicity received by findings at the [[Mae Futago Kofun|Maefutago Kofun]] which was believed at the time to have a better claim.
;Overall length: 89.9 meters ;Posterior circular portion: 44 meter diameter x 7.5 meters high, 2-tier ;Anterior rectangular portion: 61 meters wide x 8 meters high, 2-tier

{{Short description|Kofun in Japan}} {{Infobox ancient site|type = [[Zenpokoenfun]] | name =Sōja Futagoyama Kofun | native_name =総社二子山古墳 | native_name_lang = | image = Maebashi Souzya-hutagoyama Kohun 1.jpg | image_size = | alt = | caption = Sōja Futagoyama Kofun | map_type = Japan Gunma Prefecture#Japan | map_alt = location in Japan | relief = 1 | map_caption = Sōja Futagoyama Kofun | map_size = 270px | altitude_m = | altitude_ref = | coordinates = {{coord|36|24|41|N|139|2|5.3|E|region:JP|display=inline}} | map_dot_label = | location = [[Maebashi, Gunma]], [[Japan]] | region = [[Kantō region]] | type = [[kofun]] | part_of = | length = | width = | area = | volume = | diameter = | circumference = | height = | built =late 6th century | abandoned = | epochs =[[Kofun period]] | cultures = | discovered = | excavations = | archaeologists = | condition = | ownership = | management = | public_access = Yes (Park) | Historic Site = | website = | architectural_styles = | architectural_details = |notes = {{box|background=white|align=center|wide=yes|border size=3px|border color=brown|text align=center|[[Monuments of Japan|National Historic Site of Japan]]}} }}
{{nihongo|'''Sōja Futagoyama Kofun'''|総社二子山古墳|1=Sōja Futagoyama Kofun|}} is a [[Kofun period]] [[kofun|Kofun]] located in what is now the city of [[Maebashi, Gunma|Maebashi]], [[Gunma Prefecture]] in the northern [[Kantō region]] of [[Japan]]. It was designated a [[Monuments of Japan|National Historic Site of Japan]] in 1927.<ref name="Bunka1">{{cite web |url=https://bunka.nii.ac.jp/heritages/detail/171625|title=二子山古墳|language=ja |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] }}</ref> It is also sometimes referred to as the {{nihongo|'''Dantaiyama Kofun'''|男体山古墳|}}.
==Overview== Sōja Futagoyama Kofun is a {{nihongo|''[[zenpō-kōen-fun]]''|前方後円墳|lt=Zenpokoenfun|1=Zenpokoenfun|comment=doing this because I need to differentiate it in piped links too with my new interwiki tool as not the ambiguous phrase keyhole shaped kofun|1=Zenpokoenfun|1=Zenpokoenfun}}, which is shaped like a keyhole, having one square end and one circular end, when viewed from above. The tumulus is located on a plain near the southeastern slopes of [[Mount Haruna]], and is one of a cluster of ''kofun'' tumuli in the area. It has a total length of 89.9 meters and was built in two tiers, orientated to the west. The surface was originally covered in ''[[fukiishi]]'' with rows of cylindrical ''[[haniwa]]''. From the topography of the site, it is believed that the tumulus was surrounded by a moat, although this has not yet been confirmed via [[archaeological excavation]]. Although the detailed structure of the mound has not been investigated yet, it is known to have two lateral [[burial chamber]]s, one in the posterior circular portion and one in the anterior rectangular portion, but with differing construction methods. The burial chamber in the circular portion is 9.4 meters long by 3.4 meters wide and has walls made of large dressed blocked of [[:en:Andesite|Andesite]], although the ceiling has collapsed. This is one of then largest burial chambers found in Gunma Prefecture. The one in the rectangular portion is lined with natural stones and is 8.76 meters long by 2.22 meters wide. From the stonework, it is estimated that this tumulus was built in the late 6th century AD, or towards the end of the Kofun period.<ref name="Shiseki">{{cite book |last1=Isomura |first1=Yukio |last2=Sakai |first2=Hideya |title=(国指定史跡事典) National Historic Site Encyclopedia |date=2012 |publisher=学生社 |isbn=4311750404}}{{in lang|ja}}</ref>
The tomb was opened in then mid-[[:en:Edo period|Edo period]], and many [[grave goods]] including bronze and iron swords, ''[[magatama]]'' beads, gold earrings, long-necked iron jars, and [[Sue pottery]] earthenware pottery were recovered. A few of these artifacts are preserved at the [[Tokyo National Museum]], but most have been lost over the years. These include at least one sword with a silver hilt, which is mentioned in the Edo period records, but whose whereabouts are unknown.
In 1874, in response to a [[Meiji government]] project to find the grave of the legendary Toyoki-irihiko no Mikoto, the founder of [[Keno Province]] mentioned in the [[Nara period]] ''[[Nihon Shoki]]'' chronicle, Gunma Prefecture officially submitted the Sōja Futagoyama Kofun as a possible candidate. This petition was denied by the [[Imperial Household Agency]] due to lack of evidence, and due to publicity received by findings at the [[Mae Futago Kofun|Maefutago Kofun]] which was believed at the time to have a better claim.
;Overall length: 89.9 meters ;Posterior circular portion: 44 meter diameter x 7.5 meters high, 2-tier ;Anterior rectangular portion: 61 meters wide x 8 meters high, 2-tier

It was designated a [[Monuments of Japan|National Historic Site of Japan]] in 1927.<ref name="Bunka1">{{cite web |url=https://bunka.nii.ac.jp/heritages/detail/171625|title=二子山古墳|language=ja |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] }}</ref> It is also sometimes referred to as the {{nihongo|'''Dantaiyama Kofun'''|男体山古墳|}}.
==Overview== Sōja Futagoyama Kofun is a {{nihongo|''[[zenpō-kōen-fun]]''|前方後円墳|lt=Zenpokoenfun|1=Zenpokoenfun|comment=doing this because I need to differentiate it in piped links too with my new interwiki tool as not the ambiguous phrase keyhole shaped kofun|1=Zenpokoenfun|1=Zenpokoenfun}}, which is shaped like a keyhole, having one square end and one circular end, when viewed from above. The tumulus is located on a plain near the southeastern slopes of [[Mount Haruna]], and is one of a cluster of ''kofun'' tumuli in the area. It has a total length of 89.9 meters and was built in two tiers, orientated to the west. The surface was originally covered in ''[[fukiishi]]'' with rows of cylindrical ''[[:en:Haniwa|Haniwa]]''. From the topography of the site, it is believed that the tumulus was surrounded by a moat, although this has not yet been confirmed via [[archaeological excavation]]. Although the detailed structure of the mound has not been investigated yet, it is known to have two lateral [[:en:Burial chamber|Burial chamber]]s, one in the posterior circular portion and one in the anterior rectangular portion, but with differing construction methods. The burial chamber in the circular portion is 9.4 meters long by 3.4 meters wide and has walls made of large dressed blocked of [[:en:Andesite|Andesite]], although the ceiling has collapsed. This is one of then largest burial chambers found in Gunma Prefecture. The one in the rectangular portion is lined with natural stones and is 8.76 meters long by 2.22 meters wide. From the stonework, it is estimated that this tumulus was built in the late 6th century AD, or towards the end of the Kofun period.<ref name="Shiseki">{{cite book |last1=Isomura |first1=Yukio |last2=Sakai |first2=Hideya |title=(国指定史跡事典) National Historic Site Encyclopedia |date=2012 |publisher=学生社 |isbn=4311750404}}{{in lang|ja}}</ref>
The tomb was opened in then mid-[[:en:Edo period|Edo period]], and many [[:en:Grave goods|Grave goods]] including bronze and iron swords, ''[[magatama]]'' beads, gold earrings, long-necked iron jars, and [[Sue pottery]] earthenware pottery were recovered. A few of these artifacts are preserved at the [[Tokyo National Museum]], but most have been lost over the years. These include at least one sword with a silver hilt, which is mentioned in the Edo period records, but whose whereabouts are unknown.
In 1874, in response to a [[Meiji government]] project to find the grave of the legendary Toyoki-irihiko no Mikoto, the founder of [[Keno Province]] mentioned in the [[Nara period]] ''[[Nihon Shoki]]'' chronicle, Gunma Prefecture officially submitted the Sōja Futagoyama Kofun as a possible candidate. This petition was denied by the [[Imperial Household Agency]] due to lack of evidence, and due to publicity received by findings at the [[Mae Futago Kofun|Maefutago Kofun]] which was believed at the time to have a better claim.
;Overall length: 89.9 meters ;Posterior circular portion: 44 meter diameter x 7.5 meters high, 2-tier ;Anterior rectangular portion: 61 meters wide x 8 meters high, 2-tier
==See also== *[[List of Historic Sites of Japan (Gunma)]]
==References== {{reflist}}
==External links== *[https://www.maebashi-cvb.com/spot/1029 Maebashi City official guide] {{in lang|ja}}

;Overall length: 89.9 meters ;Posterior circular portion: 44 meter diameter x 7.5 meters high, 2-tier ;Anterior rectangular portion: 61 meters wide x 8 meters high, 2-tier

{{Short description|Kofun in Japan}} {{Infobox ancient site|type = [[Zenpokoenfun]] | name =Sōja Futagoyama Kofun | native_name =総社二子山古墳 | native_name_lang = | image = Maebashi Souzya-hutagoyama Kohun 1.jpg | image_size = | alt = | caption = Sōja Futagoyama Kofun | map_type = Japan Gunma Prefecture#Japan | map_alt = location in Japan | relief = 1 | map_caption = Sōja Futagoyama Kofun | map_size = 270px | altitude_m = | altitude_ref = | coordinates = {{coord|36|24|41|N|139|2|5.3|E|region:JP|display=inline}} | map_dot_label = | location = [[:en:Maebashi, Gunma|Maebashi, Gunma]], [[:en:Japan|Japan]] | region = [[:en:Kantō region|Kantō region]] | type = [[kofun]] | part_of = | length = | width = | area = | volume = | diameter = | circumference = | height = | built =late 6th century | abandoned = | epochs =[[Kofun period]] | cultures = | discovered = | excavations = | archaeologists = | condition = | ownership = | management = | public_access = Yes (Park) | Historic Site = | website = | architectural_styles = | architectural_details = |notes = {{box|background=white|align=center|wide=yes|border size=3px|border color=brown|text align=center|[[Monuments of Japan|National Historic Site of Japan]]}} }}
{{nihongo|'''Sōja Futagoyama Kofun'''|総社二子山古墳|1=Sōja Futagoyama Kofun|}} is a [[Kofun period]] [[kofun|Kofun]] located in what is now the city of [[:en:Maebashi, Gunma|Maebashi]], [[:en:Gunma Prefecture|Gunma Prefecture]] in the northern [[:en:Kantō region|Kantō region]] of [[:en:Japan|Japan]]. It was designated a [[Monuments of Japan|National Historic Site of Japan]] in 1927.<ref name="Bunka1">{{cite web |url=https://bunka.nii.ac.jp/heritages/detail/171625|title=二子山古墳|language=ja |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] }}</ref> It is also sometimes referred to as the {{nihongo|'''Dantaiyama Kofun'''|男体山古墳|}}.
==Overview== Sōja Futagoyama Kofun is a {{nihongo|''[[zenpō-kōen-fun]]''|前方後円墳|lt=Zenpokoenfun|1=Zenpokoenfun|comment=doing this because I need to differentiate it in piped links too with my new interwiki tool as not the ambiguous phrase keyhole shaped kofun|1=Zenpokoenfun|1=Zenpokoenfun}}, which is shaped like a keyhole, having one square end and one circular end, when viewed from above. The tumulus is located on a plain near the southeastern slopes of [[Mount Haruna]], and is one of a cluster of ''kofun'' tumuli in the area. It has a total length of 89.9 meters and was built in two tiers, orientated to the west. The surface was originally covered in ''[[fukiishi]]'' with rows of cylindrical ''[[:en:Haniwa|Haniwa]]''. From the topography of the site, it is believed that the tumulus was surrounded by a moat, although this has not yet been confirmed via [[archaeological excavation]]. Although the detailed structure of the mound has not been investigated yet, it is known to have two lateral [[:en:Burial chamber|Burial chamber]]s, one in the posterior circular portion and one in the anterior rectangular portion, but with differing construction methods. The burial chamber in the circular portion is 9.4 meters long by 3.4 meters wide and has walls made of large dressed blocked of [[:en:Andesite|Andesite]], although the ceiling has collapsed. This is one of then largest burial chambers found in Gunma Prefecture. The one in the rectangular portion is lined with natural stones and is 8.76 meters long by 2.22 meters wide. From the stonework, it is estimated that this tumulus was built in the late 6th century AD, or towards the end of the Kofun period.<ref name="Shiseki">{{cite book |last1=Isomura |first1=Yukio |last2=Sakai |first2=Hideya |title=(国指定史跡事典) National Historic Site Encyclopedia |date=2012 |publisher=学生社 |isbn=4311750404}}{{in lang|ja}}</ref>
The tomb was opened in then mid-[[:en:Edo period|Edo period]], and many [[:en:Grave goods|Grave goods]] including bronze and iron swords, ''[[magatama]]'' beads, gold earrings, long-necked iron jars, and [[Sue pottery]] earthenware pottery were recovered. A few of these artifacts are preserved at the [[Tokyo National Museum]], but most have been lost over the years. These include at least one sword with a silver hilt, which is mentioned in the Edo period records, but whose whereabouts are unknown.
In 1874, in response to a [[Meiji government]] project to find the grave of the legendary Toyoki-irihiko no Mikoto, the founder of [[:en:Keno Province|Keno Province]] mentioned in the [[Nara period]] ''[[Nihon Shoki]]'' chronicle, Gunma Prefecture officially submitted the Sōja Futagoyama Kofun as a possible candidate. This petition was denied by the [[:en:Imperial Household Agency|Imperial Household Agency]] due to lack of evidence, and due to publicity received by findings at the [[Mae Futago Kofun|Maefutago Kofun]] which was believed at the time to have a better claim.
;Overall length: 89.9 meters ;Posterior circular portion: 44 meter diameter x 7.5 meters high, 2-tier ;Anterior rectangular portion: 61 meters wide x 8 meters high, 2-tier

{{nihongo|'''Sōja Futagoyama Kofun'''|総社二子山古墳|1=Sōja Futagoyama Kofun|}} is a [[Kofun period]] [[kofun|Kofun]] located in what is now the city of [[:en:Maebashi, Gunma|Maebashi]], [[:en:Gunma Prefecture|Gunma Prefecture]] in the northern [[:en:Kantō region|Kantō region]] of [[:en:Japan|Japan]]. It was designated a [[Monuments of Japan|National Historic Site of Japan]] in 1927.<ref name="Bunka1">{{cite web |url=https://bunka.nii.ac.jp/heritages/detail/171625|title=二子山古墳|language=ja |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] }}</ref> It is also sometimes referred to as the {{nihongo|'''Dantaiyama Kofun'''|男体山古墳|}}.
==Overview== Sōja Futagoyama Kofun is a {{nihongo|''[[zenpō-kōen-fun]]''|前方後円墳|lt=Zenpokoenfun|1=Zenpokoenfun|comment=doing this because I need to differentiate it in piped links too with my new interwiki tool as not the ambiguous phrase keyhole shaped kofun|1=Zenpokoenfun|1=Zenpokoenfun}}, which is shaped like a keyhole, having one square end and one circular end, when viewed from above. The tumulus is located on a plain near the southeastern slopes of [[:en:Mount Haruna|Mount Haruna]], and is one of a cluster of ''kofun'' tumuli in the area. It has a total length of 89.9 meters and was built in two tiers, orientated to the west. The surface was originally covered in ''[[fukiishi]]'' with rows of cylindrical ''[[:en:Haniwa|Haniwa]]''. From the topography of the site, it is believed that the tumulus was surrounded by a moat, although this has not yet been confirmed via [[archaeological excavation]]. Although the detailed structure of the mound has not been investigated yet, it is known to have two lateral [[:en:Burial chamber|Burial chamber]]s, one in the posterior circular portion and one in the anterior rectangular portion, but with differing construction methods. The burial chamber in the circular portion is 9.4 meters long by 3.4 meters wide and has walls made of large dressed blocked of [[:en:Andesite|Andesite]], although the ceiling has collapsed. This is one of then largest burial chambers found in Gunma Prefecture. The one in the rectangular portion is lined with natural stones and is 8.76 meters long by 2.22 meters wide. From the stonework, it is estimated that this tumulus was built in the late 6th century AD, or towards the end of the Kofun period.<ref name="Shiseki">{{cite book |last1=Isomura |first1=Yukio |last2=Sakai |first2=Hideya |title=(国指定史跡事典) National Historic Site Encyclopedia |date=2012 |publisher=学生社 |isbn=4311750404}}{{in lang|ja}}</ref>
The tomb was opened in then mid-[[:en:Edo period|Edo period]], and many [[:en:Grave goods|Grave goods]] including bronze and iron swords, ''[[magatama]]'' beads, gold earrings, long-necked iron jars, and [[Sue pottery]] earthenware pottery were recovered. A few of these artifacts are preserved at the [[Tokyo National Museum]], but most have been lost over the years. These include at least one sword with a silver hilt, which is mentioned in the Edo period records, but whose whereabouts are unknown.
In 1874, in response to a [[:en:Government of Meiji Japan|Government of Meiji Japan]] project to find the grave of the legendary Toyoki-irihiko no Mikoto, the founder of [[:en:Keno Province|Keno Province]] mentioned in the [[Nara period]] ''[[Nihon Shoki]]'' chronicle, Gunma Prefecture officially submitted the Sōja Futagoyama Kofun as a possible candidate. This petition was denied by the [[:en:Imperial Household Agency|Imperial Household Agency]] due to lack of evidence, and due to publicity received by findings at the [[Mae Futago Kofun|Maefutago Kofun]] which was believed at the time to have a better claim.
;Overall length: 89.9 meters ;Posterior circular portion: 44 meter diameter x 7.5 meters high, 2-tier ;Anterior rectangular portion: 61 meters wide x 8 meters high, 2-tier
==See also== *[[:en:List of Historic Sites of Japan (Gunma)|List of Historic Sites of Japan (Gunma)]]
==References== {{reflist}}
==External links== *[https://www.maebashi-cvb.com/spot/1029 Maebashi City official guide] {{in lang|ja}}

The tumulus is located on a plain near the southeastern slopes of [[:en:Mount Haruna|Mount Haruna]], and is one of a cluster of ''kofun'' tumuli in the area. It has a total length of 89.9 meters and was built in two tiers, orientated to the west. The surface was originally covered in ''[[fukiishi]]'' with rows of cylindrical ''[[:en:Haniwa|Haniwa]]''. From the topography of the site, it is believed that the tumulus was surrounded by a moat, although this has not yet been confirmed via [[archaeological excavation]]. Although the detailed structure of the mound has not been investigated yet, it is known to have two lateral [[:en:Burial chamber|Burial chamber]]s, one in the posterior circular portion and one in the anterior rectangular portion, but with differing construction methods. The burial chamber in the circular portion is 9.4 meters long by 3.4 meters wide and has walls made of large dressed blocked of [[:en:Andesite|Andesite]], although the ceiling has collapsed. This is one of then largest burial chambers found in Gunma Prefecture. The one in the rectangular portion is lined with natural stones and is 8.76 meters long by 2.22 meters wide. From the stonework, it is estimated that this tumulus was built in the late 6th century AD, or towards the end of the Kofun period.<ref name="Shiseki">{{cite book |last1=Isomura |first1=Yukio |last2=Sakai |first2=Hideya |title=(国指定史跡事典) National Historic Site Encyclopedia |date=2012 |publisher=学生社 |isbn=4311750404}}{{in lang|ja}}</ref>
The tomb was opened in then mid-[[:en:Edo period|Edo period]], and many [[:en:Grave goods|Grave goods]] including bronze and iron swords, ''[[magatama]]'' beads, gold earrings, long-necked iron jars, and [[:en:Sue pottery|Sue pottery]] earthenware pottery were recovered. A few of these artifacts are preserved at the [[:en:Tokyo National Museum|Tokyo National Museum]], but most have been lost over the years. These include at least one sword with a silver hilt, which is mentioned in the Edo period records, but whose whereabouts are unknown.
In 1874, in response to a [[:en:Government of Meiji Japan|Government of Meiji Japan]] project to find the grave of the legendary Toyoki-irihiko no Mikoto, the founder of [[:en:Keno Province|Keno Province]] mentioned in the [[:en:Nara period|Nara period]] ''[[Nihon Shoki]]'' chronicle, Gunma Prefecture officially submitted the Sōja Futagoyama Kofun as a possible candidate. This petition was denied by the [[:en:Imperial Household Agency|Imperial Household Agency]] due to lack of evidence, and due to publicity received by findings at the [[Mae Futago Kofun|Maefutago Kofun]] which was believed at the time to have a better claim.
;Overall length: 89.9 meters ;Posterior circular portion: 44 meter diameter x 7.5 meters high, 2-tier ;Anterior rectangular portion: 61 meters wide x 8 meters high, 2-tier
==See also== *[[:en:List of Historic Sites of Japan (Gunma)|List of Historic Sites of Japan (Gunma)]]
==References== {{reflist}}
==External links== *[https://www.maebashi-cvb.com/spot/1029 Maebashi City official guide] {{in lang|ja}}

This is one of then largest burial chambers found in Gunma Prefecture. The one in the rectangular portion is lined with natural stones and is 8.76 meters long by 2.22 meters wide. From the stonework, it is estimated that this tumulus was built in the late 6th century AD, or towards the end of the Kofun period.<ref name="Shiseki">{{cite book |last1=Isomura |first1=Yukio |last2=Sakai |first2=Hideya |title=(国指定史跡事典) National Historic Site Encyclopedia |date=2012 |publisher=学生社 |isbn=4311750404}}{{in lang|ja}}</ref>
The tomb was opened in then mid-[[:en:Edo period|Edo period]], and many [[:en:Grave goods|Grave goods]] including bronze and iron swords, ''[[magatama]]'' beads, gold earrings, long-necked iron jars, and [[:en:Sue pottery|Sue pottery]] earthenware pottery were recovered. A few of these artifacts are preserved at the [[:en:Tokyo National Museum|Tokyo National Museum]], but most have been lost over the years. These include at least one sword with a silver hilt, which is mentioned in the Edo period records, but whose whereabouts are unknown.
In 1874, in response to a [[:en:Government of Meiji Japan|Government of Meiji Japan]] project to find the grave of the legendary Toyoki-irihiko no Mikoto, the founder of [[:en:Keno Province|Keno Province]] mentioned in the [[:en:Nara period|Nara period]] ''[[Nihon Shoki]]'' chronicle, Gunma Prefecture officially submitted the Sōja Futagoyama Kofun as a possible candidate. This petition was denied by the [[:en:Imperial Household Agency|Imperial Household Agency]] due to lack of evidence, and due to publicity received by findings at the [[Mae Futago Kofun|Maefutago Kofun]] which was believed at the time to have a better claim.
;Overall length: 89.9 meters ;Posterior circular portion: 44 meter diameter x 7.5 meters high, 2-tier ;Anterior rectangular portion: 61 meters wide x 8 meters high, 2-tier
==See also== *[[:en:List of Historic Sites of Japan (Gunma)|List of Historic Sites of Japan (Gunma)]]
